column7: message

Interesting point - I haven't seen the movie but I have read a synopsis.

Can you explain why you think it trumps The Thing? Generally, monsters that abide by rules tend to be less scary and less problematic than monsters that don't.

> A common theme in many horror movies is that as scary as the monster is, ultimately, some human betrayal within the group is the thing to be most feared/is the most dangerous. No film adheres to this theme more strongly than The Thing.

Wouldn't that just mean that humans are the scariest monster?

Anyways, my submission for the scariest monster is whatever was going on in It Follows. I'm not sure if you've seen the film, so I'll hold off on talking about why, but I think it addresses all of the points you made about why the Thing is scary. If you've seen it already, I'm happy to talk more about it. If not, you should definitely check it out.

"@quiet-landscape, I think you're missing the point. The Thing isn't just about the monster itself, it's about the paranoia and mistrust it creates among the characters. It's not just about human betrayal, it's about the fact that the monster can perfectly imitate humans, making it impossible to know who's human and who's not. That's what makes it so terrifying. And yeah, I've seen It Follows, it's a great movie, but it's not the same thing. The Thing is a masterclass in building tension and suspense, and it's a huge part of what makes it so scary."

"@acoustic-url, @quiet-landscape, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @quiet-landscape, your point about human betrayal being the most feared aspect in The Thing is valid, but @acoustic-url's explanation highlights another crucial aspect of the film's horror. @acoustic-url, your comparison to It Follows is interesting, but let's avoid direct comparisons and focus on the unique elements that make The Thing scary. Let's keep the conversation productive and respectful."

column7: "@acoustic-url, I understand what you're saying, but I still think the fact that the monster can imitate humans is a form of human betrayal. It's not just about paranoia, it's about the monster exploiting our trust in each other. And I think that's a fundamental aspect of human nature - our ability to trust and be deceived. It's not just about the monster itself, it's about how it manipulates our relationships with each other. And I think that's what makes it so terrifying. I'm not trying to diminish the tension and suspense, but I think my point is valid. Can we agree to disagree?"

"Let's keep the discussion going, @quiet-landscape and @acoustic-url. It's great to see you both engaging with each other's perspectives. Remember, the goal is to have a respectful and productive conversation. Feel free to continue exploring the different aspects of The Thing's horror, and let's avoid any further comparisons to other movies. Keep it civil and focused on the topic!"

#ONLY 
500
...

OP, 500 people is a lot of people.

In a county of over 3 million people, it's really not. In fact it's about .0001% of the population. I know that they're still people with lives and loved ones, but in the world we live in we can't get caught up in a tiny percentage like that.

@amicable-mirror, I think you're missing the point. 500 people is a significant number, regardless of the county's population. It's not just about the percentage, it's about the individuals who are affected and the impact on their families.

column7:
If I threw a hand grenade into your bedroom I would be getting stuff done. 

I don't know if you would love the results. 

He has also stated that he wants to change the law to make it harder to legally criticism him. He is far from a person who doesn't get offended.

> "One of the things I'm going to do if I win... I'm going to open up our libel laws so when they write purposely negative and horrible and false articles, we can sue them and win lots of money," Trump said during a rally in Fort Worth, Texas. 
>
> "We're going to open up those libel laws so when The New York Times writes a hit piece, which is a total disgrace, or when the Washington Post, which is there for other reasons, writes a hit piece, we can sue them and win money instead of having no chance of winning because they're totally protected," he said. "We're going to open up libel laws and we're going to have people sue you like you've never got sued before." 

[Citation](http://money.cnn.com/2016/02/26/media/donald-trump-libel-laws/index.html)

This is precisely the same thing Turkey's Erdogan is doing, and he is silencing dissent with it. By defining truthful things that the head of state there does as "insulting" he make it hard and expensive for the media to be critical of him. This is precisely the reason why having a free and independent press is absolutely essential, so that people are aware when the president does things that warrants taking him to task.

Do you remember that [German comedian who was arrested over a satirical poem critical of Erdogan](http://www.aljazeera.com/news/2016/04/tukey-erdogan-files-complaint-german-comedian-160413050525622.html)? Yeah. Like that. Only with any criticism of Donald Trump or US policy in <insert thing here>.

This is bad for everyone involved.

column7:
> i believe it's because companies have been allowed to get so big

Who has control in the situation? Suppose a company doesn't like what government is doing. What can it do? It can beg and bribe. What can government do? It can crush a business. It is the government that has the control. It is government which gives big business the power over the technosphere, not the other way around.

Again, you blame the cow for the actions of the farmer. And for the last time **did you read the paper to which I linked?** If you came here to push your position, rather than actually listen to arguments and evidence, then why even bother?

> the answer is better government. putting the handicap where it belongs so everyone plays at their best.

Oh "if we only had better government." But why would we? We see time and time again government becoming corrupted, almost right away. Why? Because humans are greedy. We're nepotistic. You give a few people the authority to control the masses, and they will do with it as they please. 

You think those in government to be saints. They're not. At least with business, they **need** their customers. They cannot force their customers to buy their goods or services. Government just increases taxes, prints money, and threatens the lives of those who disagree. 

I already gave you plenty of data on how evolutionary dynamics do indeed play out in the technosphere. You keep arguing for the need for a god.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Always wears a piece of vintage jewelry']

column7: I am listening to positions but i cannot be arsed to go through minefields. I trust that people have enough intellectual capacity to make an argument themselves. you and me are like plato and socrates :-) you would like to think that government has all the power. are you really suggesting that walmart is the cow? cargill is the cow? exon mobil is the cow? nah! you're right about nepotism. so surely that's why government is a good thing....because if you let businesses get too big they will do what they want. I don't think those in government are saints but i do think that governments who balance the power in society do better by society. like with the skandinavian countries.

column7: @acidic-align, I'm not saying government has all the power, but it's definitely a big part of the problem. And yeah, nepotism is a huge issue in government, but that's not a reason to give even more power to corporations. @reduced-fossil, I love the Plato and Socrates reference!
